title: Advanced Molecular Biology
description: A chapter-based ByteCafe midterm campaign for Lectures 12-22, starting with RNA-binding proteins, splicing, and alternative splicing.

modules.title: Module 12-13 - RNA Binding and Splicing
modules.description: RNA-binding proteins, splice-site recognition, and alternative splicing logic.
modules.completionLine: Module 12-13 complete. The RNA blur is turning into a mechanism you can actually explain.

modules.title: Module 14-16 - RNA Processing Systems
modules.description: rRNA/tRNA processing, capping, polyadenylation, and coordination of processing.

modules.title: Module 17-19 - RNA Fate and Translation Setup
modules.description: Turnover, nonsense-mediated decay, ribosomes, and initiation of translation.

modules.title: Module 20-22 - Silencing, Control, and Switches
modules.description: RNA interference, translational control, RNA editing, and riboswitches.

splice_builder.title: Splice Builder
learningObjectives: Identify the core players in splice-site recognition.
Use the branchpoint correctly during spliceosomal splicing.
Predict how exon choice changes the mature transcript.
splice_builder.successText: Splicing map stabilized. The transcript now makes mechanistic sense.
splice_builder.failureFeedback: The transcript is still off. Re-check recognition order, the branchpoint, and which exons survive.

rbp_docking.title: RBP Docking
learningObjectives: Match representative RNA-binding proteins to the role that makes them memorable for this module.
Distinguish splice-site support, translation support, iron-response control, and mRNA decay bias.
Turn Lecture 12 examples into functional anchors rather than isolated names.
rbp_docking.successText: Docking map locked. The factor names now point to jobs instead of floating loose in memory.
rbp_docking.failureFeedback: One or more factors are parked in the wrong lane. Re-anchor each example to its lecture role.

level_sort.title: Level Check
learningObjectives: Separate DNA-level ideas from RNA-processing events.
Distinguish mature transcript output from downstream protein consequence.
Reduce the classic exam mistake of answering at the wrong level.
level_sort.successText: Level map stabilized. You are less likely to answer a transcript question like it was a DNA question.
level_sort.failureFeedback: One or more statements are sitting at the wrong biological level.

alternative_splicing.title: Alternative Splicing Simulator
learningObjectives: Build an isoform rather than only naming it.
Predict how exon choice changes transcript or protein output.
Connect lecture examples to consequences instead of isolated terms.
alternative_splicing.successText: Isoform build confirmed. Exon choice and consequence are now linked in memory.
alternative_splicing.failureFeedback: The isoform or its consequence is off. Reconnect exon choice to output.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: Twelve to twenty-two. I do not even know why I opened all of them at once. It just... felt dishonest to ignore any of it.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Yeah, no, I opened one and my laptop made a noise like it was disappointed in me. Closed it immediately. Self-preservation.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: You are both doing that thing where you treat the whole exam like one problem. It is not.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: It kind of is. It is all connected. That is what makes it worse.

dialogue.speaker: MR. K
dialogue.text: You do not carry it all at once.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Speak for yourself. I am carrying at least six bad decisions right now.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Detected: cognitive overload across multiple modules. Recommendation: begin with Module 12-13. Your avoidance threshold is lowest there.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: ...It knows which one I am avoiding.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: Then start there.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Great. We are taking orders from the haunted computer now. Love that for us.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: No, it is... right. If I do not start somewhere, I am just going to keep sitting here thinking about how much there is.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Module 12-13 is prepared. I will adjust difficulty based on your response patterns.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: It said "response patterns" like it is already disappointed.

dialogue.speaker: MR. K
dialogue.text: Go on.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: ...Okay. Module 12-13.

m12_intro.bytosHtml:
STUDY FOCUS:
Lecture 12 gives you the control map. Lecture 13 gives you the machine. Learn them in that order.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: Lecture 12 is the kind of lecture that makes me feel stupid in a very specific way. Every slide makes sense by itself, and then five slides later I have no idea what lives where anymore.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: My notes for this section currently say, quote, 'RIP, CLIP, proteins everywhere, ask God.' So I would also like a cleaner system.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: Fine. Here is the cleaner system. First: what RNA-binding proteins are doing in the broadest sense. Second: how binding works well enough for the course. Third: what the methods actually let you claim. Then you earn the right to touch splicing.

dialogue.speaker: MR. K
dialogue.text: People make late-night studying harder than it already is. You do not need to understand the whole building. You need the next room with the light on.

m12_methods_scene.bytosHtml:
CHECKPOINT:
You are past the first panic wall. Keep the question simple: what kind of evidence does each method give you?

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: Okay, this is better. I do not need to memorize every step of every protocol. I need to know what kind of answer RIP gives me, what kind of answer CLIP gives me, and why those are not interchangeable.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Yes. Exactly. RIP is like, 'these RNAs and this protein were seen together in public,' and CLIP is more like, 'no, seriously, this thing was actually binding here.' Which is still not scientific language, but it is a start.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: It is a better start than whatever you were doing before. Methods matter because they limit the claim. If you remember the shape of the claim, the method usually follows.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: That might be the first time this section has felt finite.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Recommendation: bind names to jobs next. Representative proteins are easier to keep when each one owns a lane.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: Hold on... it is not random. It looks messy, but it is not. It is steps. It always goes in steps.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Yeah, but the steps have names like U1, U2. Who named these, a Wi-Fi router?

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: The names do not matter. The order does. You lose the order, you lose everything.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: U1 finds it first. Then U2 comes in, like it confirms it. And then the rest just... builds around that.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: So it is like assembling a team. One guy points, one guy nods, then suddenly there is a whole operation happening.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: It is not a team. It is a sequence. Each step only makes sense because of the last one.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: No, I get what she means. If I skip ahead, it stops making sense. But if I follow it... it actually flows.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: You are transitioning from memorization to process tracking. This is required for accuracy.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Process tracking sounds like something I would fail immediately.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: You will now perform it. Interactive sequence initialized.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: ...Okay. Yeah. Let us try it.

m12_pressure_scene.bytosHtml:
ADJUST:
Check what level you are answering from.
{WEAK_SPOT_LINE}

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: I know exactly where I am messing up now. I keep answering like it is just sequence, like it is only the letters. And then the question shifts and I am already wrong.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Yeah, that is my specialty. I see something familiar and I just... commit. Full confidence, zero verification. It is a lifestyle.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: You are answering at the wrong level. You are staying at sequence when the question moved to structure.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: It does not feel like it moved. That is the problem. It looks the same and then suddenly it is not.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: That is how they get you. It is like those trick questions where you are already halfway through being wrong before you realize it.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: It is not a trick. It is layered. You just did not switch layers.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: So I need to... check what the question is actually asking before I lock in.

dialogue.speaker: MR. K
dialogue.text: Slow it down a notch.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Slowing down is terrifying, by the way. That is when I realize I do not know things.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: You already do not. You are just noticing it earlier.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: ...Okay. Next one, I am going to pause. Just actually read it properly.

dialogue.speaker: MR. K
dialogue.text: Good.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: I do not need to be fast. I just need to stop answering the wrong question.

m12_complete.bytosHtml:
COMPLETE:
Keep what holds when it is messy.
Run grade: {PERF_LABEL}. {STRONGEST_LINE}

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: It is not clean. I still hesitate on half of it. But I can actually follow it now... even when it shifts.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: That is a win. I am calling it. I am doing a small, respectful victory dance internally because Mr. K is watching.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: Do not call it anything yet. Just notice that you did not fall apart when it got harder.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: Yeah. Before, one weird question and I would lose the whole thread. Now it just... bends a bit.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Flexible brain. We love to see it. Mine snaps immediately, so this is aspirational.

dialogue.speaker: MR. K
dialogue.text: You stayed with it.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: I think that is the first time tonight I did not feel like I was guessing.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: Good. Then it is yours now.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Module 12-13 complete. Your error patterns have narrowed. Residual weaknesses persist.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Of course they do. Keeps things interesting.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: What is next?

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Module 14-16 is ready. It will build directly on this. Your current state is sufficient, but fragile.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: Then do not let it cool off too long.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: No break? Not even a celebratory snack break?

dialogue.speaker: MR. K
dialogue.text: Five minutes.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: ...Yeah. Five minutes. Then we keep going.

m12_checkpoint_scene.bytosHtml:
CHECKPOINT:
Clarity under pressure reveals what stays.
{WEAK_SPOT_LINE}

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Checkpoint initiated. Integrated assessment. I will increase pressure until your pattern breaks.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Cool. Love that wording. Super normal thing to say to a human being.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: No, it is fine. I just... need to see if it still holds when it is not clean.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: Then do not hesitate. First answer only. If you start second-guessing, you are done.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Okay, but if I black out mid-question, tell my family I tried.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: All right. Read it once. Do not overthink. Figure out what level it is asking.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Your previous errors were clustered around misapplied levels of analysis. This will be tested.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: ...Okay. It is not sequence. It is interaction. No, structure affecting interaction.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: Commit.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: I am committing.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Correct.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Yes. First hit. We are alive.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: Do not celebrate. Next one.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Increasing complexity.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Of course you are.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: ...This one is trying to make me rush.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: It is not. You are.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: Right. Pause. What is it actually asking... okay, process, not outcome.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Correct. Your response latency increased, but error probability decreased.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Slow and correct. That is a rebrand for me, honestly.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: Last one.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Final question will combine all prior concepts.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: Boss phase two. I knew it.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: ...Okay. It is mixing splicing and binding. So I cannot treat them separately.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: Good. Keep going.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: Binding affects which sites are recognized... which changes the outcome. So it is upstream control.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Correct.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: He did it. Full clear.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: ...Okay. That actually held. Even when it got messy.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Checkpoint complete. Your understanding remains intact under increased cognitive load.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: Good. Now you can trust it.

dialogue.speaker: MR. K
dialogue.text: Take a breath.

m14_intro.scene: CHAPTER 7 - THERE IS AN ORDER TO THIS
m14_intro.bytosHtml:
NEXT ARC:
Module 14-16 is scaffolded but not fully playable yet. This scene marks the unlock and where the next build should go.

dialogue.speaker: MR. K
dialogue.text: Most difficult material gets easier the same way. First it is noise. Then it is parts. Then one night the order shows up.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: That was the first chapter, I think. Not mastery. Just... a route. Like I could finally see where to stand.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Future module queued: RNA processing systems. Content not yet authored. Narrative expectation: increasing order, increasing fatigue.

m17_intro.bytosHtml:
FUTURE ARC:
Module 17-19 is planned for RNA fate, decay, ribosomes, and initiation.

dialogue.speaker: DOUGIE
dialogue.text: This is the stage of the night where caffeine stops being a beverage and becomes a belief system.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Future module scaffold active. Expected mood: fatigue, false confidence, and a dangerous interest in ribosomes.

m20_intro.bytosHtml:
FUTURE ARC:
Module 20-22 is planned for RNA interference, translational control, editing, and riboswitches.

dialogue.speaker: NADIA
dialogue.text: By the last chapter the course stops being impressed that you know names. It wants to know whether you understand regulation as a system.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Future module scaffold active. Expected emphasis: control logic, outcomes, and pattern recognition under pressure.

midterm_finale.bytosHtml:
FINAL SCENE:
The full midterm campaign is not built yet, but this is the intended emotional endpoint.

dialogue.speaker: REN
dialogue.text: I still do not know everything. But it does not feel like standing in front of a wave anymore.

dialogue.speaker: MR. K
dialogue.text: Nobody ever walks into an exam feeling complete. You just want to arrive steady.

dialogue.speaker: BYTE/OS
dialogue.text: Session complete. Rest is now more valuable than one additional panicked hour.

methods: You described what was found, but not what the method allows you to claim.
You treated absence of signal as absence of interaction. That is not supported here.
Your answer ignores how the interaction was preserved before isolation.
You are assuming stability where the method only captures what survives handling.
You answered as if all interactions are equally detectable. They are not.
You identified the interaction, but not whether it would persist without intervention.
rbp_logic: You focused on the RNA sequence but ignored what the protein is selecting for.
You treated binding as passive. It changes what happens next.
You recognized the motif, but not why it matters functionally.
You assumed binding is uniform. It is selective and conditional.
You identified where binding occurs, but not its consequence.
You are describing presence, not control.
splice_mechanism: You skipped a step. The order is what gives the mechanism meaning.
You identified the components but not when they act.
You treated recognition as simultaneous. It is sequential.
You placed the branchpoint incorrectly in the process.
You described the outcome without accounting for how it forms.
You are compressing multiple steps into one. That is where the error begins.
self_splicing: You merged the mechanisms. They are similar in outcome, not in execution.
You treated external factors as required when they are not.
You identified the product, but not how it is generated.
You assumed protein involvement where the RNA acts alone.
You collapsed two distinct pathways into one explanation.
You focused on structure, but missed the catalytic difference.
level_effects: You answered at the sequence level when the question asked about outcome.
You jumped to protein consequences without resolving RNA processing first.
You are mixing levels. Separate them before choosing.
You assumed a DNA change when the variation occurs after transcription.
You identified the mechanism but not where its effect appears.
You are describing what exists, not what changes.
alt_splicing: You treated exon variation as a mutation. It is a choice, not a change.
You focused on presence of sequence, not its inclusion.
You assumed one transcript. There are multiple valid outcomes.
You identified the exon, but not whether it is kept or skipped.
You described the gene, not the isoform.
You are treating variability as error. It is regulated.
checkpoint: You committed before identifying what the question was asking.
You recognized a pattern and stopped checking it.
You answered too quickly for the level of integration required.
You collapsed distinct ideas into one under pressure.
You did not pause to confirm the level of analysis.

m12_rbp_overview.label: RBP Core
m12_rbp_overview.html:
RNA-BINDING PROTEINS

Lecture 12 reframes gene expression as regulation across the full RNA and protein life cycle, not just transcription initiation.

  • RNA-binding proteins act on pre-mRNA, mature mRNA, non-coding RNA, localization, decay, translation, and more.
  • There are over two thousand RBPs with diverse roles.
  • Post-transcriptional control lets cells respond faster than waiting to remake transcriptional programs from scratch.

Memory hook: RBPs are not one niche topic. They are a recurring control layer across RNA life.

m12_interactions.label: Binding Logic
m12_interactions.html:
HOW RBPS BIND RNA

The lecture uses interaction principles to make RNA-protein binding feel less magical.

  • Hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions help proteins read RNA shape and chemistry.
  • Base stacking and aromatic contacts can stabilize RNA recognition.
  • Specificity comes from a combination of structure, sequence context, and domain architecture rather than one universal rule.
  • RNA-recognition motifs and other domains should be remembered as functional tools, not just vocabulary.

Exam hook: when asked why one protein binds one region better than another, think interaction logic plus context, not one single bond.

m12_methods.label: Methods
m12_methods.html:
RIP, CLIP, AND INTEGRATION

Lecture 12 emphasizes that methods answer different questions about RNA-protein interactions.

  • RIP: immunoprecipitate an RBP and identify associated RNAs.
  • CLIP: crosslink RNA-protein interactions first, then recover binding relationships with stronger positional confidence.
  • Mass spectrometry, bioinformatics, and other assays help turn binding data into functional claims.
Method Lens
Pick a method lens to reinforce what each one preserves.

Exam-safe distinction: methods show association and binding logic, but interpreting function usually requires integration with additional data.

m13_splicing_core.label: Splicing Core
m13_splicing_core.html:
CANONICAL SPLICING

Pre-mRNA contains exons and introns. The spliceosome removes introns and ligates exons.

  • U1 recognizes the 5-prime splice site.
  • U2 recognizes the branchpoint.
  • U2AF helps define the 3-prime splice region.
  • U5 helps align exons, while U6 participates in catalytic rearrangement.
  • The first reaction uses the 2-prime OH of the branchpoint A to form a lariat intermediate.

Memory hook: 5-prime site, branchpoint, 3-prime region, lariat, exon ligation.

m13_self_splicing.label: Group I vs II
m13_self_splicing.html:
SELF-SPLICING VS SPLICEOSOMAL LOGIC

Lecture 13 uses self-splicing to show that intron removal chemistry can be organized in different ways.

  • Group I introns use an external guanosine nucleophile to begin the first transesterification.
  • Group II introns use an internal branchpoint adenosine and form a lariat-like intermediate.
  • Spliceosomal splicing is mechanistically closer to Group II logic than to Group I.

Exam hook: if you see external guanosine, think Group I. If you see branchpoint A and lariat, think Group II or spliceosomal logic.

m13_alt_splicing.label: Alt Splicing
m13_alt_splicing.html:
ALTERNATIVE SPLICING

One DNA gene can yield multiple mature mRNAs through different exon choices.

  • Alternative splicing does not change genomic DNA.
  • It can change coding sequence, UTRs, localization, stability, or protein activity.
  • SR proteins and other RNA-binding factors help commit transcripts to particular splice patterns.
  • Examples from the lecture include Drosophila sex determination, FOXP1 isoforms, and extreme Dscam diversity.
Level Check
Move through the ladder to keep DNA, processing, transcript, and protein effects separate.
DNAProcessingTranscriptProtein

Exam-safe distinction: the output changes because RNA processing choices change, not because the gene itself mutates.

future_processing_note.label: Next: Processing
future_processing_note.html:
MODULE 14-16 PREVIEW

This module is unlocked in the roadmap but not fully authored yet. It will cover rRNA/tRNA processing, capping, polyadenylation, and coordinated processing.

future_fate_note.label: Next: Fate
future_fate_note.html:
MODULE 17-19 PREVIEW

This future module will cover turnover, nonsense-mediated decay, ribosomes, and initiation of translation.

future_control_note.label: Next: Control
future_control_note.html:
MODULE 20-22 PREVIEW

This future module will cover RNA interference, translational control, RNA editing, and riboswitches.

posts.author: Ren
posts.subject: If you start too wide, it never starts
posts.body: I opened everything at once tonight and it just... flattened me. It all looked important and none of it stuck. I had to close everything and pick one thread. Not the easiest one, the one that kept showing up. That helped more than I expected. Still tired though.

posts.author: Dougie
posts.subject: midterm strategy (patent pending)
posts.body:
step 1: panic a little (sets the tone)
step 2: open ONE lecture only
step 3: pretend it is the only lecture that exists in the universe
step 4: do NOT think about the others waiting in the dark
step 5: reward yourself with a snack you did not earn

I am on step 5. Highly recommend.

posts.author: Nadia
posts.subject: Stop treating it like a pile
posts.body:
It is not a pile of facts. It is a set of systems that repeat with variation.
If you cannot see the pattern, you are still looking at the surface.
Pick one mechanism. Follow it until it shows up somewhere else.
Then you are actually studying.

posts.author: BYTE/OS
posts.subject: Observation: entry behavior
posts.body:
Most users delay starting until anxiety decreases.

Anxiety does not decrease before starting.

Initiating work reduces uncertainty. Reduced uncertainty lowers perceived anxiety.

The sequence is often misunderstood.

posts.author: Ren
posts.subject: RIP vs CLIP finally clicked (kind of)
posts.body: I kept mixing them up until I stopped trying to memorize definitions. RIP is what survives. CLIP is what was there. That shift helped a lot. Still messy, but I can actually tell them apart now when I see a question.

posts.author: Dougie
posts.subject: CLIP is basically evidence locker mode
posts.body:
RIP = "who is still holding hands when the lights come on"
CLIP = "freeze everyone mid-conversation, nobody move"

if this shows up on the exam I am writing it exactly like this and hoping for partial credit

posts.author: Nadia
posts.subject: The mistake you are making
posts.body:
Most of you are answering at the wrong level.
You see a sequence and assume that is the question.
It usually is not.
Ask what is being tested: sequence, structure, interaction, or outcome.
If you do not switch levels, you will keep getting the same type of question wrong.

posts.author: BYTE/OS
posts.subject: Pattern update
posts.body:
User errors in this module frequently cluster around:
- overreliance on sequence signals
- failure to integrate binding context

Targeted variation will be introduced in subsequent prompts.

You will recognize this pattern when it is presented again.

posts.author: Dougie
posts.subject: who turned the lights dimmer after 1am
posts.body:
not accusing anyone but the cafe definitely gets 20% more existential after midnight
like the chairs are quieter
my thoughts are louder
and the computer is starting to feel like it knows me personally

anyway if I disappear I have simply become part of the furniture

posts.author: Ren
posts.subject: it gets quieter around this time
posts.body: There is a point where everyone stops talking and you can just hear keyboards and the hum from the back machines. It is not peaceful exactly. Just... focused. Like everyone decided to stay, even if they are tired.

posts.author: Nadia
posts.subject: Observation
posts.body:
People either leave before this hour or become very serious.
There is not much in between.
It is easier to work when fewer people are pretending.

posts.author: Mr. K
posts.subject: Still Open
posts.body: If you are still here, you are doing enough.

posts.author: BYTE/OS
posts.subject: Module pending
posts.body: Processing module unlocked. Content authoring still in progress.

posts.author: BYTE/OS
posts.subject: Module pending
posts.body: RNA fate module scaffolded. Content authoring still in progress.

posts.author: BYTE/OS
posts.subject: Module pending
posts.body: Control systems module scaffolded. Content authoring still in progress.

puzzles.title: MODULE 12-13 - REGULATION IS NOT JUST ON/OFF
puzzles.desc: Which statement best matches Lecture 12's big-picture definition of gene expression regulation?
puzzles.bytosTip:
FRAME THE WHOLE CHAPTER:
Lecture 12 says regulation happens across the gene product life cycle, not just at transcription initiation.

choices.label: Gene expression is mostly just turning transcription on or off.
Regulation can occur before, during, and after transcription, as well as during translation and beyond.
Only proteins regulate gene expression meaningfully.
Post-transcriptional regulation is too slow to matter.

puzzles.canonicalAnswer: Regulation can occur across the full gene product life cycle.
puzzles.explanation: The lecture explicitly argues against a simple on-off view. Cells regulate gene expression before transcription initiation, during RNA processing, during translation, and after protein production too.
puzzles.learningObjective: Reframe gene expression control as a multi-stage system.

puzzles.title: MODULE 12-13 - IRON RESPONSE CASE
puzzles.desc: In iron deficiency, what is the useful Lecture 12-level takeaway from the IRP example?
puzzles.bytosTip:
CASE MODE:
Use the iron-response example to remember that RBPs can alter transcript fate after transcription, not just gene transcription itself.

choices.label: IRP proves that all RNA-binding proteins only affect transcription initiation.
IRP illustrates how RNA-binding factors can change mRNA stability or translation depending on context.
IRP is mainly a splicing factor that removes introns.
The example shows that RNA-binding proteins only matter in bacteria.

puzzles.canonicalAnswer: IRP shows that RNA-binding factors can regulate transcript stability or translation depending on the RNA context.
puzzles.explanation: The iron-response example is memorable because it shows post-transcriptional control in action. The broader exam-safe lesson is that RBPs change RNA fate after transcription by binding specific transcript features.
puzzles.learningObjective: Use the IRP example as a model of post-transcriptional control.

puzzles.title: MODULE 12-13 - METHOD MATCH
puzzles.desc: In one short phrase, what is the main study-level purpose of CLIP in RBP research?
puzzles.bytosTip:
METHOD HOOK:
Think binding evidence with stronger positional confidence after crosslinking, not full protocol detail.
acceptedAnswers: identify where an rbp binds rna
find rna binding sites for an rbp
show where a protein binds rna
determine where a protein binds rna
puzzles.canonicalAnswer: CLIP is used to identify where an RNA-binding protein binds RNA.
puzzles.explanation: At the exam-relevant level, CLIP is the method memory hook for locating RNA-protein binding interactions with stronger site-oriented confidence than a vague association answer.
puzzles.learningObjective: Differentiate the purpose of CLIP from broader RBP evidence collection.

puzzles.title: RBP DOCKING - FUNCTION LANES
puzzles.desc: Dock representative factors into the lane that best matches their memorable role in this module.
puzzles.bytosTip:
MINIGAME:
Use the examples as anchors. Each factor should map to one lane you can actually explain out loud.

failureFeedbackMap.splice_site_definition: That lane is about splice-site definition. Re-anchor the factor that helps define the 3-prime splice region.
failureFeedbackMap.translation_support: This lane is the translation-support memory hook. Think poly(A)-binding protein, not a splicing or decay factor.
failureFeedbackMap.iron_response_stability: That lane should point back to the Lecture 12 iron-response example rather than a general RNA fate factor.
failureFeedbackMap.mrna_decay_bias: This lane is about a factor associated with transcript-decay bias in the lecture examples, not splice-site recognition or translation support.

puzzles.canonicalAnswer: U2AF -> splice-site definition; PABP -> translation support; IRP -> iron-response control; YTHDF2 -> mRNA decay bias.
puzzles.explanation: The point of the board is not memorizing four names in isolation. It is attaching each representative factor to a role you can retrieve under exam pressure.
puzzles.learningObjective: Turn Lecture 12 example proteins into functional anchors.

puzzles.title: MODULE 12-13 - SPLICE SITE RECOGNITION ORDER
puzzles.desc: Build the simplified recognition logic for spliceosomal splicing.
puzzles.bytosTip:
SEQUENCE MODE:
Keep it study-friendly: who recognizes which region before catalytic rearrangement?

sequenceSteps.label: U2AF helps define the 3-prime splice region
U6 replaces U1 and participates in catalytic rearrangement

puzzles.explanation: The key exam habit is linking the 5-prime site, branchpoint, 3-prime region, and catalytic handoff into one coherent mechanism instead of memorizing disconnected factor names.
puzzles.learningObjective: Recall the core recognition order of spliceosomal splicing.

puzzles.title: MODULE 12-13 - FIRST CHEMICAL ATTACK
puzzles.desc: Which feature launches the first transesterification step during canonical spliceosomal splicing?
puzzles.bytosTip:
CHEMISTRY HOOK:
The first attack uses the branchpoint adenosine. Do not swap the branchpoint with a splice-junction label.

choices.label: The 3-prime AG dinucleotide attacks first.
The branchpoint adenosine provides the 2-prime OH for the first attack.
A DNA polymerase starts the reaction.
The mature mRNA cap launches the first attack.

puzzles.canonicalAnswer: The branchpoint adenosine provides the 2-prime OH for the first transesterification.
puzzles.explanation: This is the lariat-forming step. The branchpoint A matters because its 2-prime OH attacks the 5-prime splice site, creating the characteristic lariat intermediate.
puzzles.learningObjective: Tie branchpoint recognition to the actual chemistry of lariat formation.

puzzles.title: MODULE 12-13 - GROUP I VS GROUP II
puzzles.desc: Which comparison best matches the lecture's distinction between Group I and Group II self-splicing introns?
puzzles.bytosTip:
COMPARE MODE:
External guanosine is the Group I giveaway. Branchpoint A and lariat logic point toward Group II.

choices.label: Group I uses an external guanosine, while Group II uses an internal branchpoint adenosine and lariat-like chemistry.
Group I and Group II both require U1, U2, U5, and U6 snRNPs in the same way.
Group II uses DNA polymerase to remove introns, while Group I edits the chromosome.
There is no useful mechanistic distinction between Group I and Group II.

puzzles.canonicalAnswer: Group I uses an external guanosine, while Group II uses an internal branchpoint adenosine and lariat-like chemistry.
puzzles.explanation: That comparison is the exam-safe anchor. Group I is remembered through the external guanosine nucleophile, whereas Group II is remembered through branchpoint-A and lariat logic that resembles spliceosomal chemistry.
puzzles.learningObjective: Keep Group I and Group II chemistry distinct.

visual.alt: Group I versus Group II intron comparison

puzzles.title: MODULE 12-13 - ALTERNATIVE SPLICING CONSEQUENCE
puzzles.desc: What is the clearest exam-safe consequence of alternative splicing?
puzzles.bytosTip:
DO NOT CONFUSE THIS:
Alternative splicing changes transcript output. It does not rewrite genomic DNA.

choices.label: It permanently edits the gene in the chromosome.
It can generate different mature mRNAs and protein isoforms from one gene.
It removes the need for the spliceosome.
It only matters in fruit flies.

puzzles.canonicalAnswer: Alternative splicing can generate different mature mRNAs and protein isoforms from one gene.
puzzles.explanation: The lecture emphasizes that alternative splicing changes RNA output and can alter coding sequence, UTRs, localization, stability, and protein activity without altering the underlying DNA sequence.
puzzles.learningObjective: Separate alternative splicing consequences from DNA-level changes.

puzzles.title: SPLICE BUILDER - CANONICAL ROUND
puzzles.desc: Build a canonical splicing outcome from pre-mRNA recognition through exon ligation.
puzzles.bytosTip:
MINIGAME:
Get the recognition order, branchpoint, and surviving exons all right in one run.

config.prompt: Create the mature transcript produced by standard spliceosomal removal of the middle intron.

puzzles.canonicalAnswer: Recognize the splice sites correctly, use the branchpoint A, and keep exon 1 plus exon 2.
puzzles.explanation: Canonical splicing removes the intron, forms a lariat through the branchpoint A, and ligates the flanking exons into the mature mRNA.
puzzles.learningObjective: Integrate splice-site recognition, branchpoint use, and exon ligation in one playable mechanism.

puzzles.title: SPLICE BUILDER - EXON SKIP ROUND
puzzles.desc: Build an alternative splicing outcome where the middle exon is skipped.
puzzles.bytosTip:
MINIGAME:
Alternative splicing changes transcript output. Build the isoform, not a DNA mutation.

config.prompt: Produce the exon-skipped isoform that joins exon 1 directly to exon 3.

puzzles.canonicalAnswer: Recognize the splice sites correctly, use the branchpoint A, and keep exon 1 plus exon 3.
puzzles.explanation: Alternative splicing can change the mature RNA by altering exon inclusion. The genome stays the same; the processed transcript changes.
puzzles.learningObjective: Apply splicing logic to an exon-skipping outcome.

puzzles.title: LEVEL CHECK - WHAT CHANGES, EXACTLY?
puzzles.desc: Sort each statement into the biological level it belongs to before exam pressure scrambles them together.
puzzles.bytosTip:
LEVEL CHECK:
This chapter gets much easier once DNA, processing, transcript output, and protein consequence stop bleeding into each other.

items.prompt: A promoter mutation changes the sequence present in the gene before transcription begins.
items.feedback: That statement is about the DNA template itself, not RNA processing or transcript output.

items.prompt: Exon 2 is skipped during intron removal while the underlying gene sequence stays the same.
items.feedback: That statement is about pre-mRNA processing choices, not a DNA mutation.

items.prompt: The mature mRNA now lacks exon 2 and joins exon 1 directly to exon 3.
items.feedback: That statement describes the mature transcript that comes out after processing.

items.prompt: The translated protein loses a domain and localizes differently in the cell.
items.feedback: That statement is downstream of RNA processing. It is a protein-level consequence.

puzzles.canonicalAnswer: DNA change vs processing event vs mature transcript output vs protein consequence.
puzzles.explanation: This is the exam habit that saves points. A lot of wrong answers are not fully ignorant, just one level off.
puzzles.learningObjective: Separate DNA-level changes from RNA-processing and protein-output consequences.

puzzles.title: ALTERNATIVE SPLICING SIMULATOR - ISOFORM BUILD
puzzles.desc: Assemble the isoform that best fits the case, then choose the outcome that matches the lecture logic.
puzzles.bytosTip:
ISOFORM BUILD:
Do not just name alternative splicing. Build the isoform and tie it to a consequence.

config.prompt: A regulator biases the transcript toward skipping exon 2. Build the resulting isoform and choose the best study-safe consequence.

outcomes.label: Same gene, different mature mRNA/protein isoform

puzzles.canonicalAnswer: Skip exon 2 and predict a new mature mRNA/protein isoform from the same gene.
puzzles.explanation: Alternative splicing is powerful because the same gene can produce different outputs without rewriting the DNA template.
puzzles.learningObjective: Connect exon choice to the resulting isoform and consequence.

puzzles.title: CHECKPOINT - PICK THE RIGHT METHOD
puzzles.desc: You want stronger positional confidence about where an RBP contacts RNA. Which method is the best memory-match?
puzzles.bytosTip:
CHECKPOINT ROUND:
The chapter boss is mixed on purpose. Stay calm and answer at the right level.

choices.label: CLIP
A generic transcription assay
DNA sequencing of the chromosome only
No method can answer that question

puzzles.explanation: This is the method checkpoint: CLIP is the stronger memory hook for positional RNA-binding evidence.

puzzles.title: CHECKPOINT - WHAT STARTS THE LARIAT?
puzzles.desc: In one short phrase, what feature begins the first spliceosomal attack that forms the lariat intermediate?
puzzles.bytosTip:
CHECKPOINT ROUND:
Short answer now. Keep it tight and mechanistic.
acceptedAnswers: 2 prime oh of the branchpoint adenosine
puzzles.canonicalAnswer: The branchpoint adenosine provides the nucleophile for the first attack.
puzzles.explanation: The branchpoint adenosine is the lariat anchor. If that is stable in memory, the mechanism stops feeling arbitrary.
puzzles.learningObjective: Recall the core chemistry of lariat formation under pressure.

puzzles.title: CHECKPOINT - WHAT DID NOT CHANGE?
puzzles.desc: A question describes exon skipping that produces a different isoform. What is the safest thing to say did NOT change?
puzzles.bytosTip:
FINAL CHECKPOINT:
Last hit. Do not lose the level distinction now.

choices.label: The mature mRNA output
The resulting protein isoform
The underlying genomic DNA sequence
The splice pattern itself

puzzles.canonicalAnswer: The underlying genomic DNA sequence did not change.
puzzles.explanation: This is the chapter's core guardrail. Alternative splicing changes RNA output and often protein output, not the gene sequence itself.
puzzles.learningObjective: Hold the DNA-versus-RNA distinction even at the end of the run.

completion.title: MIDTERM ARC COMPLETE
completion.summary: You cleared the currently authored midterm modules. Review weak spots, then extend the remaining chapter modules.
completion.bytosLine: Current campaign slice complete. Additional modules remain scaffolded for future study passes.
